items.q: What is the key structural difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?
items.correct: Eukaryotes have a membrane-bound nucleus and membrane-bound organelles; prokaryotes do not.
items.misconception: Prokaryotes have no DNA — that's why they have no nucleus.
items.random_wrong: Prokaryotes are always smaller than 1 micrometre and have no cytoplasm.
items.why: Students see 'no nucleus' and think 'no DNA', missing that prokaryotes still have DNA — it's just free in the cytoplasm.

items.q: What happens to an enzyme when it is denatured?
items.correct: Its tertiary structure (3D shape) changes, particularly the active site, so it can no longer bind substrate effectively.
items.misconception: The enzyme has died because enzymes are alive.
items.random_wrong: The enzyme has been completely broken into amino acids.
items.why: Students think of enzymes as alive (probably because they are produced by living things), so they describe denaturation as death rather than shape change.

items.q: What is the main role of mitochondria in a cell?
items.correct: To carry out aerobic respiration, producing ATP that cells use as an energy currency.
items.misconception: Mitochondria produce energy by creating it from nothing.
items.random_wrong: Mitochondria store sunlight and release it for photosynthesis.
items.why: The phrase 'powerhouse of the cell' makes students think energy is created by mitochondria, missing that they transfer energy from glucose to ATP.

items.q: Do mitochondria create energy from nothing?
items.correct: No — mitochondria release energy already stored in glucose during aerobic respiration; energy is transferred, not created.
items.misconception: Mitochondria make brand-new energy for the cell.
items.random_wrong: Mitochondria store sunlight for the cell to use.
items.why: Students say cells 'make' energy, but energy cannot be created; respiration converts the chemical energy in glucose into usable ATP.

items.q: Does high temperature always speed up an enzyme reaction?
items.correct: No — rate rises with temperature only up to the optimum; above it the enzyme denatures and the rate falls.
items.misconception: The hotter it gets, the faster an enzyme always works.
items.random_wrong: Enzymes work best at 0°C because cold preserves them.
items.why: Students apply 'hotter = faster' without limit, but beyond the optimum the active site loses its shape and activity drops sharply.

items.q: Which organelle is the site of aerobic respiration?
items.why: It's Mitochondrion. “Ribosome” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Ethanol and CO₂. “Lactic acid” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Roughly how many ATP molecules per glucose in aerobic respiration?
items.why: The real figure is 32. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
